lesson.text: You cannot secure what you do not see.

Attack surface defined: every point where an attacker can interact with a system. Village framing — gates, walls, sewer grates, fords — and their digital equivalents: endpoints, APIs, login forms, forgotten admin panels.
Defense-in-Depth — layered PREVENTATIVE (stop), DETERRENT (discourage), and DETECTIVE (observe & alert) controls. A system relying on prevention alone fails silently.

Capability mapping: real threat modeling defends against the SPECIFIC actors capable of exploiting SPECIFIC gaps, not "everyone." Reference the Saboteur: high dexterity and stealth versus an unwatched lock on a cliff path.
In the field you saw this as the West Gate. In an audit you will see it as an unlogged service endpoint behind a default password.

quiz.q: A castle has a moat, guard dogs, and a logbook at the door. Which is the DETECTIVE control?
explain: Preventative — it stops the approach before it starts.
Deterrent — it discourages the attempt without necessarily recording it.
Detective — a logbook observes and creates an audit trail after the fact.

quiz.q: Your web app has a login page, a public API, and an old admin panel nobody remembers. Which is part of the attack surface?
explain: The attack surface isn't just the obvious front door.
The API matters, but it isn't the whole picture.
Attack surface = every interaction point, ESPECIALLY the forgotten ones — that is your West Gate.

quiz.q: Why did the Saboteur — not the Sorcerer — threaten the West Gate?
explain: Raw strength isn't what mattered here.
Threat modeling maps actor capability to system gap; strength without fit is noise.
A colorful guess, but not the actual reasoning.
